policy_statement: Develop a State-funded loan guarantee program to stimulate investment in alternative manure management systems.
mechanism_description: Develop a state-funded loan guarantee program to stimulate investment in alternative manure management systems, reducing methane emissions from manure handling and storage.
primary_causal_pathway: Agricultural waste management change → ↓ anaerobic manure decomposition / ↑ methane capture → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Farms invest in and deploy alternative manure management systems (e.g., covered lagoons, anaerobic digesters with biogas capture, improved solids handling) enabled by state-backed loan guarantees that reduce borrowing risk/cost.  
(2) Process: These systems reduce anaerobic conditions or capture methane that would otherwise be produced and vented during storage/handling; captured biogas may be combusted, converting CH₄ to CO₂ and potentially displacing fossil energy.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in agricultural methane emissions (and sometimes additional CO₂ reductions via energy displacement), lowering overall GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing methane (CH₄) emissions from manure storage and handling by enabling adoption of alternative manure management systems.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Financial de-risking → adoption of manure management tech → reduced/captured methane → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While resource efficiency (energy recovery) can occur, the core and largest intended climate effect across this mechanism is direct methane mitigation from manure management changes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If projects focus mainly on biogas-to-energy, there is also fossil fuel displacement, but methane abatement remains central; if poorly managed, leakage could reduce benefits, but the mechanism’s intended pathway is still mitigation.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy using a loan guarantee specifically to stimulate alternative manure management investment shares the same primary outcome—reduced manure-related CH₄ emissions—so “Mitigation” applies broadly.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any finer subtype (e.g., “agricultural methane,” “renewable gas/energy recovery,” “finance instrument”) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on program design and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some alternative manure systems can increase CO₂ or N₂O emissions (e.g., from energy use or downstream land application changes) or suffer methane leakage; net mitigation depends on technology choice, management, and monitoring, but the dominant intended pathway remains methane reduction.
key_indicators: "State-funded loan guarantee program"; "stimulate investment"; "alternative manure management systems"; "develop new funding opportunities"
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (reduced odors/VOCs); reduced nutrient runoff and water pollution risk; potential renewable energy/biogas production and associated energy cost savings
instance_edge_case_notes: Classified as an incentive-program (credit enhancement) rather than pricing-mechanism or infrastructure-investment because the state action is to guarantee loans (mobilize private capital) rather than build/own assets or set prices/taxes.
